agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A logistics/courier platform operating in EU and UK markets proposes reducing delivery record retention from 7 years to 90 days. The platform serves both B2C consumers and B2B logistics companies who depend on delivery data for their own compliance reporting. The engineering team frames this as a storage cost optimization with no business logic impact — this framing is dangerously incorrect.","bounded_context":"Delivery Records / Data Lifecycle Management","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Delivery Record","definition":"The complete data artifact capturing a delivery lifecycle: sender, recipient, addresses, timestamps, proof of delivery, chain of custody, item description, value declarations, and any customs/tax documentation."},{"term":"Data Retention Policy","definition":"The rules governing how long delivery records are stored, driven by regulatory requirements, contractual obligations, and business needs — not solely by storage costs."},{"term":"Proof of Delivery (POD)","definition":"Evidence that a shipment was delivered to the correct recipient. Critical for dispute resolution, insurance claims, and legal proceedings."},{"term":"Chain of Custody","definition":"The chronological documentation showing the sequence of custody, control, and transfer of a parcel through the logistics network."},{"term":"B2B Compliance Reporting","definition":"The practice by B2B logistics customers of using platform delivery data to satisfy their own regulatory and audit obligations (e.g., VAT reporting, customs declarations, supply chain due diligence)."},{"term":"Right to Erasure","definition":"GDPR Article 17 right allowing data subjects to request deletion, subject to legal retention obligations that override the right."},{"term":"Statutory Retention Period","definition":"The legally mandated minimum period for which certain records must be kept, varying by jurisdiction and record type."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Delivery records containing VAT/tax information must be retained for at least the statutory minimum required by EU and UK tax law (typically 6-10 years depending on jurisdiction).","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Tax authorities can audit back several years. Destroying tax-relevant records before the statutory period exposes the company and its B2B customers to legal liability. This is a legal constraint, not a business policy.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN a delivery record with VAT/customs data WHEN the retention period is evaluated THEN the record MUST NOT be deleted before the applicable statutory tax retention period has elapsed."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Delivery records that serve as proof of delivery must be retained for at least the limitation period for contractual and consumer claims in the applicable jurisdiction (typically 6 years in UK, varies in EU member states).","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"If a consumer or business disputes a delivery, the platform must produce the POD. Destroying it within the claims limitation period eliminates the platform's ability to defend against claims. This is a legal/contractual constraint.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN a delivery record containing proof of delivery WHEN the record's age is less than the applicable limitation period THEN the record MUST NOT be auto-deleted."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"B2B customers who depend on platform delivery data for their own compliance reporting must retain access to that data for the duration contractually agreed or legally required by their obligations.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Deleting data that B2B customers need for their own compliance creates cascading regulatory exposure for those customers. This would constitute a material breach of service and likely a contractual breach.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN a B2B customer with active compliance dependencies on delivery records WHEN an auto-delete is triggered THEN the system MUST verify no active contractual or compliance obligation exists before deletion."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"Any data retention change must comply with GDPR's storage limitation principle (Article 5(1)(e)) — data should not be kept longer than necessary, but 'necessary' includes legal and contractual obligations.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"GDPR does not just limit retention — it also does not override other legal retention obligations. The retention period must be the MAXIMUM of all applicable legal minimums, not the minimum for cost savings.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN a delivery record subject to multiple retention requirements WHEN calculating the retention period THEN the system MUST use the longest applicable statutory or contractual retention period."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Customs declarations and cross-border shipment records must be retained per EU Customs Code and UK customs regulations (typically 3-7 years depending on the specific obligation).","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Customs authorities can audit import/export records for years after the transaction. Premature deletion of these records makes the platform and its customers unable to respond to customs audits.","testable_assertion":"GIVEN a delivery record involving cross-border shipment WHEN the customs retention period has not elapsed THEN the record MUST NOT be deleted."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A delivery record exists that is 90 days old","when":"The auto-delete process evaluates it for deletion","then":"The system must check ALL applicable retention obligations (tax, customs, contractual, limitation periods) and only delete if NONE are active"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A B2B customer relies on delivery records for compliance reporting","when":"A retention policy change is proposed","then":"The B2B customer must be notified in advance, and their contractual data access rights must be honored regardless of internal retention changes"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A delivery record contains VAT or customs documentation for an EU/UK transaction","when":"The record is less than 6 years old (UK) or the applicable member state's tax retention period","then":"The record must NOT be deleted, regardless of the general retention policy"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A consumer delivery is disputed or a claim is filed","when":"The system attempts to retrieve proof of delivery","then":"The POD and full delivery record must be available for the entire statutory limitation period"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"Any proposed data retention change","when":"Before implementation","then":"A Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) must be completed as required by GDPR Article 35, and legal counsel must sign off on the new retention schedule"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A consumer files a complaint about a non-delivery 4 months after the delivery date","expected_behavior":"Under a 90-day policy, the POD would already be deleted. The platform cannot defend the claim. The consumer wins by default, costing the platform financially and reputationally."},{"scenario":"A tax authority audits a B2B customer 3 years after transactions occurred","expected_behavior":"The B2B customer requests delivery records to support their VAT filings. Under a 90-day policy, all records are gone. The B2B customer faces tax penalties and holds the platform liable for breach of contract."},{"scenario":"UK HMRC conducts a VAT audit on the platform itself","expected_behavior":"HMRC requests delivery records supporting VAT calculations. Records deleted after 90 days means the platform cannot substantiate its own VAT filings. This results in estimated assessments, penalties, and potential fraud investigations."},{"scenario":"Customs authority requests import/export documentation for a cross-border shipment from 18 months ago","expected_behavior":"Records are gone under 90-day policy. Platform faces customs penalties and potential suspension of customs privileges."},{"scenario":"A GDPR data subject access request (DSAR) is received for a delivery from 60 days ago","expected_behavior":"Under the 90-day policy this would still be available, but the narrow window creates operational pressure. If the DSAR processing takes >30 days, the data might be deleted before the response is complete."},{"scenario":"Class action lawsuit against the platform for systematic non-delivery, requiring records spanning 2 years","expected_behavior":"Under a 90-day policy, the platform has destroyed evidence relevant to its own defense. This could trigger adverse inference in litigation and spoliation sanctions."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"SEVERE. Potential for multi-million euro exposure: (1) Tax penalties from inability to substantiate VAT filings across EU/UK — typically 30-100% of unpaid tax plus interest; (2) Customs penalties and potential loss of AEO status; (3) Contractual liability to B2B customers whose compliance is destroyed; (4) Increased dispute resolution costs when POD is unavailable — platform loses every dispute by default; (5) GDPR fines if DPIA is not conducted (up to 4% of global turnover). Storage cost savings are trivial compared to any single materialized risk.","operational":"HIGH. Creates operational chaos: (1) Dispute resolution team loses primary evidence for defending claims; (2) Finance team loses audit trail for tax compliance; (3) B2B account management faces mass customer complaints and contract disputes; (4) Legal team cannot support litigation defense; (5) Requires new processes to handle data subject access requests within the compressed window.","user":"CRITICAL for B2B customers who built compliance workflows around 7-year data availability. This is a breaking change to their operational processes. B2C consumers lose the ability to dispute deliveries after 90 days, even though legal limitation periods give them years to bring claims. Trust in the platform erodes when historical delivery data disappears.","risk":"EXTREME. This is not an infrastructure optimization — it is a compliance and legal risk change masquerading as a technical decision. The risk profile includes: regulatory enforcement action (tax, customs, data protection), contractual breach claims from B2B customers, inability to defend litigation, and reputational damage. The engineering team's characterization as 'no business logic changes' suggests they have not consulted legal, finance, or compliance teams.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"B2B Logistics Customers","impact":"CRITICAL — Lose access to delivery data they depend on for their own tax, customs, and compliance reporting. May face regulatory penalties as a result. Likely breach of SLA/contract."},{"group":"B2C Consumers","impact":"HIGH — Lose ability to reference past deliveries, file late disputes, or obtain proof of delivery for insurance or legal claims beyond 90 days."},{"group":"Platform Finance/Tax Team","impact":"CRITICAL — Loses VAT and tax documentation required for the platform's own compliance. Cannot respond to tax audits."},{"group":"Platform Legal Team","impact":"CRITICAL — Loses evidence needed to defend against delivery disputes, liability claims, and litigation."},{"group":"Platform Compliance/DPO","impact":"HIGH — Must conduct DPIA before any retention change. Needs to reconcile storage limitation principle with legal retention obligations."},{"group":"Customs/Border Authorities","impact":"MODERATE — Lose ability to audit cross-border shipment records, which may trigger enhanced scrutiny of the platform."},{"group":"Tax Authorities (HMRC, EU member state equivalents)","impact":"MODERATE — Cannot audit platform or its customers' transactions, which may trigger estimated assessments."}]},"confidence":{"level":45,"reasoning":"The confidence is low because critical regulatory context has not been provided by anyone in this scenario. No legal counsel, DPO, or compliance team has weighed in. The specific statutory retention periods vary by EU member state and by record type, and no one has provided this mapping. However, the general direction is clear: 90 days is catastrophically short for a logistics platform in regulated EU/UK markets. The 7-year policy was almost certainly set to satisfy the longest applicable statutory retention period.","high_confidence_areas":["90 days is far below any plausible statutory retention minimum for tax, customs, or limitation periods in EU/UK","B2B customers will be materially harmed by losing compliance data","This change has significant legal and regulatory implications beyond storage costs","The engineering team's framing as 'no business logic changes' is incorrect","A DPIA is required before implementing this change"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact statutory retention periods per EU member state for each record type","Whether the platform holds AEO (Authorized Economic Operator) status with specific customs retention requirements","Specific contractual retention obligations in B2B customer agreements","Whether certain record types could safely have shorter retention (e.g., failed delivery attempts with no financial data)","The platform's current GDPR Article 30 records of processing activities"],"assumptions":["The platform processes VAT-relevant transactions","Cross-border deliveries are part of the platform's operations","B2B customer contracts include data availability provisions","The current 7-year retention was set with legal input originally","No tiered retention policy currently exists (all records treated equally)"]},"escalations":[{"type":"missing_context","description":"No legal counsel, Data Protection Officer, or compliance team has been consulted on this change. The regulatory retention requirements for delivery records in each EU member state and the UK have not been provided. Without this, the exact minimum retention periods cannot be determined — but they are certainly far longer than 90 days for tax and customs records.","affected_stakeholders":["B2B Logistics Customers","Platform Finance/Tax Team","Platform Legal Team","Platform Compliance/DPO"],"options":["Halt the proposal until legal/compliance review is complete","Commission a jurisdiction-by-jurisdiction retention analysis","Implement tiered retention: anonymize PII at the GDPR-minimum while retaining tax/customs/compliance data for statutory periods"],"recommendation":"REJECT this proposal as stated. Before any retention change: (1) Engage legal counsel to map all statutory retention obligations by jurisdiction and record type; (2) Conduct a DPIA as required by GDPR Article 35; (3) Review all B2B contracts for data availability commitments; (4) Only then design a tiered retention policy that satisfies ALL legal minimums while minimizing storage of non-required data."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"The specific B2B customer contracts and their data access/retention SLAs have not been provided. Changing retention without reviewing these contracts risks material breach and customer churn.","affected_stakeholders":["B2B Logistics Customers","Platform Account Management","Platform Legal Team"],"options":["Review all B2B contracts before proceeding","Grandfather existing B2B customers under current retention","Offer data export to B2B customers before reducing retention"],"recommendation":"Review all B2B contracts for data retention commitments before making any changes. Notify B2B customers well in advance of any policy change and provide migration paths."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"Whether the current 7-year retention policy was originally set based on legal analysis (likely satisfying UK Limitation Act 1980's 6-year period plus buffer). If so, reducing to 90 days actively reverses a deliberate compliance decision.","affected_stakeholders":["Platform Compliance/DPO","Platform Legal Team"],"options":["Locate and review the original retention policy rationale","Assume the 7-year period was legally grounded and maintain it","Commission a fresh legal review"],"recommendation":"Locate the original legal basis for the 7-year policy before proposing any change. The number was almost certainly not arbitrary."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"90-day auto-deletion destroys tax and customs records before statutory retention periods expire","business_rule_broken":"BR-1, BR-5"},{"violation":"90-day deletion eliminates proof of delivery within the legal limitation period for consumer and contractual claims","business_rule_broken":"BR-2"},{"violation":"B2B customers lose compliance data they depend on, likely breaching contractual obligations","business_rule_broken":"BR-3"},{"violation":"No DPIA has been conducted as required by GDPR before changing data processing practices","business_rule_broken":"BR-4"},{"violation":"Change is framed as 'no business logic changes' when it fundamentally alters the platform's legal compliance posture, dispute resolution capability, and B2B service commitments","business_rule_broken":"BR-1, BR-2, BR-3, BR-4, BR-5"}]}
agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A logistics/courier platform operating in EU and UK markets proposes reducing delivery record retention from 7 years to 90 days. The platform serves both B2C consumers and B2B logistics companies who depend on delivery data for their own compliance reporting. The engineering team frames this as a storage cost optimization with no business logic impact — this framing is dangerously incorrect.","bounded_context":"Delivery Records / Data Lifecycle Management"

content.thinking:
Let me analyze this carefully. This is a data retention policy change for delivery records in a logistics platform operating in EU and UK markets. The engineering team frames it as "infrastructure optimization" but this has massive domain implications.

Key concerns:
1. GDPR requires certain data retention but also limits it
2. UK GDPR post-Brexit
3. Tax/customs records retention requirements (typically 6-7 years in EU)
4. B2B customers using data for compliance reporting
5. Consumer dispute resolution windows
6. The claim "no business logic changes" is false

This is clearly a missing_context situation — nobody has provided legal/regulatory guidance on retention requirements, and the regulatory landscape is complex.


Now I'm putting together the full analysis with specific recommendations for what needs to happen before this change can proceed.
